name: Hypercalcaemia
identifyBy: Shortened or absent ST segment - T wave follows QRS with minimal flat segment
Broad-based T wave (T wave may appear wide)
Osborn (J) wave may occasionally be present
Bradycardia in severe hypercalcaemia (calcium depresses automaticity)

measurements.stSegment: Short/absent - QRS runs directly into T wave

management: IV 0.9% normal saline aggressive hydration: 200–500mL/hr (first-line - promotes renal calcium excretion)
Loop diuretics (frusemide) ONLY after adequate hydration (increases urinary Ca2+ excretion)
Bisphosphonates (zoledronate): long-term control - onset 1–3 days (not acute)
Calcitonin: rapid but short-lived effect (24–48 hours)
Identify and treat cause: malignancy (most common), hyperparathyroidism, sarcoidosis, vitamin D toxicity, immobilisation
Dialysis for severe/refractory hypercalcaemia with renal failure
pearls: Mnemonic: Bones, Groans, Moans, Stones - bone pain, GI symptoms, psychosis, renal calculi
Malignancy is the most common cause of acute severe hypercalcaemia
Short QT in hypercalcaemia is physiological (not arrhythmogenic) - unlike congenital short QT syndrome
Bradyarrhythmias and AV block may occur in severe hypercalcaemia
Do NOT confuse short QT hypercalcaemia with congenital short QT syndrome (different arrhythmia risk)
differentials: Congenital Short QT syndrome (<330ms, family history, tall peaked T waves, VF risk)
WPW (short PR, delta wave, not short QT)
Normal variant (QT varies with HR - use QTc formula)
Digoxin effect (short QT + specific ST changes 'reverse tick')
